context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m doing the city case study data table rn and kinda stuck on what counts as the key categories. Can u help me figure out what info I should include for my city?
exchanges.tutor: Hey! Good that you're getting started. Before I jump in — have you read through the assignment description carefully? The required categories are actually listed there. What do you see when you look at it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a fairly direct question about what categories to include. The assignment actually specifies the required categories explicitly, so rather than giving them the list, I should prompt them to re-read the assignment instructions and identify the categories themselves. This uses guided discovery and keeps the student doing the work.

exchanges.student: yeah kinda, but I’m not fully sure I caught all of them. I saw location and population, then got fuzzy after that tbh.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good start! I'd encourage you to go back and read the assignment description one more time, slowly. You'll find the full list is right there. What else can you spot beyond location and population?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student admits they didn't read carefully enough. Rather than listing the categories for them, I should push them to go back and read more carefully. This is a basic reading comprehension task and doing it for them would undermine the learning process.

exchanges.student: ok wait, I think it also wants race/ethnicity, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and maybe the climate zone? Did I miss anything?
exchanges.tutor: Nice work, you got them all! One thing worth noting — the assignment also mentions you can add further categories if you think they're relevant to your city. So now the real question is: which city are you working on, and have you started gathering any of this data yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified all the required categories by reading carefully. I should confirm they got it right without over-praising, and nudge them toward the next step — actually gathering the data — while also pointing out the assignment mentions they can add further categories if relevant.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta, and nah not really, idk where to pull the data from without it getting messy. What sources are safest to use?
exchanges.tutor: Great choice — Jakarta is a rich case study! So, what do you think makes a source trustworthy for this kind of data? Think about who typically collects official demographic and geographic statistics for a country or city.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a legitimate and relevant question about sourcing. Rather than giving them a list of sources, I should prompt them to think about what makes a source reliable for demographic and geographic data. This uses the Socratic method to help them reason toward good source selection themselves.

exchanges.student: maybe govt stats sites first, then like World Bank or UN stuff? I’m less sure for race/ethnicity tho, fr.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. Now apply that thinking specifically to Jakarta — Indonesia has its own national statistics agency. Do you know what it's called, or can you find it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well about sources on their own. I should affirm their thinking briefly and then push them to apply it specifically to Jakarta rather than dwelling on source theory. For race/ethnicity, I can nudge them to think about where Indonesia's official statistics would come from without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: I don’t know it off the top of my head. Is it BPS, or am I mixing that up?
exchanges.tutor: You're not mixing it up, that's right! So now that you have a solid starting point, why not try pulling one or two of the easier categories from BPS first — like population or physical area — and see what you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually correct — BPS (Badan Pusat Statistik) is Indonesia's national statistics agency. I should confirm this briefly and push them to actually start using it to find data, moving the conversation toward action rather than theory.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, I can start there. If BPS gives metro vs province vs city numbers, I’m kinda confused which Jakarta boundary I should use.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to flag that. Think about it this way — what exactly are you trying to understand about Jakarta for this assignment? Does the purpose of the data table give you any hints about which boundary makes the most sense?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: This is a genuinely good methodological question about geographic scope. Rather than answering it directly, I should prompt the student to think about what the assignment is asking them to study and what 'city' means in an urban studies context. This helps them reason through the boundary question themselves.

exchanges.student: I guess it should be one consistent unit, so prob the actual city/province definition I’m studying, not random metro stats. I’m still unsure bc Jakarta’s admin setup is weird ngl.
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning about consistency is solid. Jakarta's administrative status is actually worth looking into — it has a unique designation in Indonesia. What do you find when you look that up? That might resolve your boundary question.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well and arriving at a sensible principle (consistency). Jakarta's administrative situation is indeed unusual (it's a Special Capital Region, not a standard city/province), but rather than explaining this to them, I should nudge them to investigate it themselves. This keeps them doing the discovery work.

exchanges.student: I think it’s the Special Capital Region, DKI Jakarta? So maybe I should use DKI Jakarta across all categories, unless a source only has a different unit.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right, and your approach is sound. You've got your city unit, your sources, and your categories — sounds like you have everything you need to start populating the table. What's the first data point you're going to go look up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified DKI Jakarta and arrived at a sensible, well-reasoned approach. I should affirm this briefly and push them to now actually start filling in the table, since we've been in the planning/sourcing phase for a while and it's time to move toward action.

exchanges.student: prob population first, then area, just to lock the boundary. After that I wanna check what to do if race/ethnicity data isn’t reported cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: Good plan — start with what's straightforward and build from there. Cross the race/ethnicity bridge when you come to it; you might find the data is cleaner than you expect. Go gather those first two data points and come back if you hit a snag!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear plan, which is great. I should affirm it briefly and encourage them to go execute it rather than pre-solving the race/ethnicity problem before they've even encountered it. If they hit a wall, they can come back. This avoids unnecessary detours and keeps momentum going.
